VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: This is the navigation information a VOR provides to a pilot.
choices: Distance to the station in nautical miles
The aircraft's altitude above the station
The magnetic radial from the station (bearing information)
A voice broadcast of the station's weather
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: VOR provides bearing — the radial from the station — not distance, altitude, or weather. Distance is provided by DME (Distance Measuring Equipment), often collocated with a VOR. Weather broadcasts are a separate service.

VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: This is the function of the Omni-Bearing Selector (OBS) on a VOR indicator.
choices: To tune the VOR station's frequency
To display the aircraft's current altitude
To control the VOR station's signal strength
To select a specific radial to or from the VOR station
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: The OBS selects the desired radial. The CDI then shows deviation from that radial. Station tuning is done at the nav radio, not the OBS. The OBS does not display altitude. Pilots cannot control the station's transmitted signal strength.

VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: Compared to VOR, this describes ADF/NDB navigation in general aviation.
choices: Generally less accurate and less reliable than VOR
More accurate than VOR under all conditions
Requires GPS augmentation to function
Only used for precision approaches
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: ADF/NDB's low-frequency signal is more susceptible to interference than VOR's VHF signal. VOR is the more accurate and reliable navigation aid. ADF does not need GPS and is used for non-precision approaches and en route navigation, not precision approaches.

VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: This is the system that provides both vertical and horizontal guidance to pilots during landing approaches.
choices: VOR
ADF/NDB
DME
ILS (Instrument Landing System)
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: The Instrument Landing System (ILS) combines the localizer (horizontal, centered on the runway) with the glideslope (vertical descent guidance) for precision approaches. VOR and ADF provide bearing only, not vertical guidance. DME provides distance, not guidance.

VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: This is what a marker beacon provides during an ILS approach.
choices: Horizontal course guidance to the runway centerline
Distance-from-runway position fixes at defined points along the approach path
Vertical descent guidance
Voice broadcast of runway conditions
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: Marker beacons provide position fixes — the aircraft overflies them at known points and the pilot knows precisely where they are along the approach. Horizontal guidance comes from the localizer. Vertical guidance comes from the glideslope. Voice broadcasts are a separate ATIS/AWOS service.

VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: This is the primary purpose of an audio control panel in an aircraft.
choices: To boost the transmit power of the VHF radio
To allow the pilot to select which radios to transmit and monitor
To convert AC power for avionics use
To regulate cabin pressure
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: The audio panel is a source-selection and routing device. It does not amplify transmit power (that is the radio's transmitter), convert power, or regulate pressurization.

VOR & ILS Navigation.clue: This is the information DME provides to the pilot.
choices: Slant-range distance (in nautical miles) from the aircraft to the ground station
The magnetic bearing from the station
The weather at the station
The runway heading at the destination airport
VOR & ILS Navigation.explanation: DME provides distance — specifically slant-range, not horizontal distance. Bearing comes from VOR. Weather is an ATIS/AWOS broadcast. Runway information is from the approach chart, not DME.

GPS & WAAS.clue: This is the minimum number of satellites a GPS receiver must track for a three-dimensional position fix.
GPS & WAAS.explanation: Four satellites are the minimum for a 3D fix — three for spatial triangulation and a fourth to resolve receiver clock bias. Two satellites cannot produce a position fix. Three can produce only a 2D fix (assuming known altitude). Eight is typical for modern receivers tracking more than the minimum, but it is not the minimum requirement.

GPS & WAAS.clue: This is what the acronym WAAS stands for.
choices: Wireless Altitude Assurance Source
Wide Angle Approach Sensor
Weather Augmentation Assistance System
Wide Area Augmentation System
GPS & WAAS.explanation: WAAS is the Wide Area Augmentation System — FAA-developed GPS augmentation for improved accuracy and integrity in North America.

VHF Communications.clue: This is the optimal mounting location for a VHF communication antenna on a GA aircraft.
choices: On the fuselage (top or bottom) with a clear line of sight in all directions and proper airframe ground plane beneath the base
Inside the cockpit near the pilot
Attached to the propeller hub
On the inside surface of the windshield
VHF Communications.explanation: Fuselage mounting (top or bottom) with clear line of sight and proper ground plane is the correct approach. Interior mounting is shielded by the airframe. Propeller mounting is unworkable. Windshield mounting lacks a ground plane.

VHF Communications.clue: This is what the squelch control does in a VHF radio system.
choices: Boosts the transmit power of the radio
Selects the operating frequency
Controls the audio panel's intercom volume
Suppresses the receiver audio output during periods of no signal or weak signal below a threshold
VHF Communications.explanation: Squelch mutes the receiver audio when there is no signal (or signal below threshold), preventing constant background hiss. It does not affect transmit power, frequency selection, or intercom functions.

VHF Communications.clue: When replacing a coaxial cable for a communication antenna, this is CRUCIAL.
choices: Use any coax available, regardless of type
Splice multiple shorter coax runs together to save cost
Ignore bend radius as long as the cable fits
Match the characteristic impedance (typically 50 ohms) and use the correct cable type
VHF Communications.explanation: Impedance match and correct cable selection are fundamental. Using the wrong impedance (75 ohm video coax instead of 50 ohm comm coax, for example) causes reflections and power loss. Splicing and ignoring bend radius both introduce losses and potential damage that degrade antenna system performance.

VHF Communications.clue: This is what regular inspection of aircraft communication antennas should include.
choices: Only the connector at the avionics rack end of the coaxial cable
Replacing the antenna every 12 months regardless of condition
Adjusting the antenna's transmit frequency
Checking for physical damage, base corrosion, mounting hardware condition, sealant integrity, and bonding/grounding condition
VHF Communications.explanation: Antenna inspection covers physical, mounting, and bonding aspects. Only checking the far-end connector misses the antenna itself. Antennas are replaced when inspection reveals damage, not on a fixed interval. Antennas do not have tunable transmit frequencies — they are passive radiators.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: This transponder mode transmits both the four-digit identification code and the aircraft's pressure altitude.
choices: Mode A
Mode S only
Mode C
No mode transmits altitude
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: Mode C adds altitude reporting to the identification code Mode A already provides. Mode S is a more advanced standard that includes everything Mode C does plus unique addressing and data link; however, the specific mode that FIRST added altitude reporting to the basic ID function is Mode C.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: This is the function of the 24-bit address in a Mode S transponder.
choices: It uniquely identifies the airframe and enables selective interrogation and ADS-B identification
It encodes the current squawk code
It indicates the transponder's data rate
It stores the aircraft's GPS position
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: The 24-bit ICAO address is a unique airframe identifier. The squawk code is a separate four-digit pilot-settable code. Transponder data rate is fixed by the standard. GPS position comes from the GPS receiver, not the transponder address.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: According to FAR 91.413, this is how often an aircraft transponder must be tested and inspected.
choices: Every 12 calendar months
Every 36 calendar months
Every 24 calendar months
Every 100 flight hours
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: FAR 91.413 requires transponder test and inspection at least every 24 calendar months. Annual (12-month) and 36-month intervals are not the required cadence. Flight-hour intervals do not apply to this requirement.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: The transponder squawk code is set by the pilot to do this.
choices: Select the transponder's operating frequency
Determine the aircraft's altitude reporting resolution
Program the 24-bit Mode S address
Identify the aircraft to ATC, often with controller-assigned codes for flight tracking or reserved codes for emergencies
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: The squawk code identifies the aircraft to ATC. Controllers assign unique codes to track flights individually. Reserved codes communicate specific conditions (emergency, radio failure, hijack). Transponder frequency is fixed by the standard. Altitude reporting resolution is also fixed. The 24-bit address is a separate, permanent Mode S identifier.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: This is the typical mounting location for an aircraft transponder antenna.
choices: On the top of the vertical stabilizer
On the leading edge of the wing
Inside the cockpit next to the pilot
On the underside (belly) of the aircraft
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: Transponder antennas go on the belly because ATC radar is below the aircraft. Vertical stabilizer and wing leading-edge locations are used for other antennas (VOR, nav/strobe) but not transponders. The antenna cannot be inside the cockpit — the airframe would block the signal.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: This is what ADS-B Out broadcasts to ATC and other aircraft.
choices: Only the aircraft's ICAO address
GPS-derived position, velocity, altitude, ICAO address, and identification
The pilot's voice transmissions
Weather radar imagery
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: ADS-B Out is a continuous broadcast of position, velocity, altitude, and identification data. It is not voice communication and does not carry weather radar imagery — those are separate systems.

Transponders & Surveillance.clue: This is what the acronym TCAS stands for.
choices: Tactical Combat Avoidance Sensor
Traffic Collision Avoidance System
Terminal Control Approach Structure
Thrust Control Activation Subsystem
Transponders & Surveillance.explanation: TCAS is Traffic Collision Avoidance System (sometimes expanded as Traffic Alert and Collision Avoidance System).

Audio & Intercom.clue: In general-aviation aircraft audio systems, this describes typical 'mono' audio use.
choices: Only one earpiece of the headset receives audio; the other is silent
Mono refers to audio broadcast on AM only
The same audio signal is sent to both earpieces of the headset, which is standard for voice communications and intercom
Mono audio is obsolete and no longer used
Audio & Intercom.explanation: Mono audio sends the same signal to both ears — standard for voice traffic that does not benefit from stereo imaging. One-ear-only is not the definition of mono. Mono is not tied to AM broadcast. It remains current practice in GA audio panels.

Audio & Intercom.clue: This is a common cause of intermittent or noisy audio in an aircraft communication system.
choices: Poor or broken grounding, corroded connectors, broken cable shields, loose mic/headset jack contacts, or nearby EMI sources
The radio is tuned to the wrong frequency
The pilot is wearing the wrong type of headset
The audio panel has too many sources selected
Audio & Intercom.explanation: Intermittent/noisy audio is typically a physical connection problem — grounding, connectors, shields, or jack contacts. Wrong frequency would produce no received audio at all, not noise. Headset type and source selection are secondary factors.

final.clue: A Mode S transponder differs from Mode A/C by featuring unique selective addressing. How many bits are in a discrete Mode S ICAO aircraft address?
choices: 12-Bit
24-Bit
32-Bit
64-Bit
final.explanation: Every Mode S equipped aircraft is assigned a unique 24-bit ICAO address, hardcoded or strapped into the transponder, allowing over 16 million distinct addresses.

final.clue: A VOR receiver determines bearing to a station by measuring the phase difference between a reference phase signal and this type of signal.
choices: A variable directional phase signal
A paired DME pulse pair
A 1090 MHz reply
An ILS localizer tone
final.explanation: VORs transmit a 30Hz reference signal (omnidirectional) and a 30Hz variable signal (sweeping directional). The receiver compares their phase difference to determine the specific radial.

final.clue: The Wide Area Augmentation System (WAAS) provides GPS corrections to the aircraft via this specific method.
choices: VHF datalink from ground stations
Geostationary satellites transmitting on the L1 frequency
Cellular LTE networks
Mode S Extended Squitter
final.explanation: WAAS ground stations calculate GPS error corrections, uplink them to geostationary (GEO) satellites, which broadcast the corrections back to aircraft using the standard GPS L1 frequency.
